docs.title: Python Fragments
docs.text:
Modern HTML template rendering in Python - no build step, no template files, and native HTML awareness out of the box.

Plain PythonFastAPIFlaskDjango 
# main.py
from fragments import loader

from views import render_index

# views.py
from my_components import Layout, PostCard

POSTS = [...]  # your data here

def render_index() -> str:
    published = [p for p in POSTS if p.published]
    return <>
        <Layout title="My Blog">
            <h1>Latest Posts</h1>
            <PostCard for={{ post in published }} post={{ post }} />
        </Layout>
    </>

# main.py
from fragments import loader

from fastapi import FastAPI
from .index import router

app = FastAPI()
app.include_router(router)

# index.py
from fastapi import APIRouter
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse

router = APIRouter()

POSTS = [...]  # your data here

@router.get("/", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def index() -> str:
    published = [p for p in POSTS if p.published]
    return <>
        <Layout title="My Blog">
            <h1>Latest Posts</h1>
            <PostCard for={{ post in published }} post={{ post }} />
        </Layout>
    </>

# app.py
from fragments import loader

from flask import Flask
from views import index_bp

app = Flask(__name__)
app.register_blueprint(index_bp)

# views.py
from flask import Blueprint

index_bp = Blueprint("index", __name__)

POSTS = [...]  # your data here

@index_bp.route("/")
def index() -> str:
    published = [p for p in POSTS if p.published]
    return <>
        <Layout title="My Blog">
            <h1>Latest Posts</h1>
            <PostCard for={{ post in published }} post={{ post }} />
        </Layout>
    </>

# urls.py
from fragments import loader

from django.urls import path
from views import index

urlpatterns = [path("", index)]

# views.py
from django.http import HttpRequest, HttpResponse

POSTS = [...]  # your data here

def index(request: HttpRequest) -> HttpResponse:
    published = [p for p in POSTS if p.published]
    return HttpResponse(<>
        <Layout title="My Blog">
            <h1>Latest Posts</h1>
            <PostCard for={{ post in published }} post={{ post }} />
        </Layout>
    </>)

docs.title: No build step
docs.text: Transpiled automatically at import time. No CLI, no watchers, no separate compile phase.

docs.title: Full IDE Support
docs.text: Type checking, hover, completions, go-to-definition, rename, and semantic highlighting via a built-in language server and VS Code extension.

docs.title: Production Tested
docs.text: Used in production and developed in-house by TheRunningAlgorithm, exposed to thousands of live users prior to open source release

docs.title: Long Term Support
docs.text: Actively maintained by TheRunningAlgorithm, who use it in production.

docs.title: 1. Install
docs.text:
pip install python-fragments

Register the loader at your app's entry point, before importing any modules that use fragments:

from fragments import loader
from my_components import my_component

Any .py file containing <> is transpiled automatically — nothing else to configure.

Full installation guide

docs.title: 2. Use With Your IDE
docs.text:
Install the language server for inline type errors, completions, and hover docs:

pip install python-fragments[lsp]

A VS Code extension is included with structural syntax highlighting, semantic tokens, diagnostics, go-to-definition, rename, and more.

Full IDE setup guide

docs.title: Benchmarks
docs.text:
Two benchmarks are included: one measuring the cost of transpilation (a one-time import-time cost), and one measuring the cost of calling a transpiled function at runtime.

All results were measured on a single machine and will vary by hardware. Run the benchmarks yourself to get numbers for your environment.

docs.title: Transpilation
docs.text:
Measures how long transpile() takes to convert a fragments source file into Python. This cost is paid once per file at import time, not on every request.

Source: benchmarks/transpilation/sample.py: a realistic file with two components, a for loop, interpolations, and an HTML comment.

python benchmarks/transpilation/benchmark.py

Transpiler benchmark (1000 iterations × 5 repeats)
  Average per call: 134.9 µs

A simple file like this can be transpiled fully 7,400 times per second. At a typing speed of 400 characters per minute (fast, 6 characters per second, 1 character every 0.15 seconds) the file you're working on can be transpiled 1,200 times per character typed - this will never be the limiting factor in LSP or IDE performance.

docs.title: Calls
docs.text:
Measures how long a transpiled fragment function takes to execute at runtime compared to an equivalent plain string interpolation. This is the cost paid on every request.

Source: benchmarks/calls/sample.py: two functions that each render a list of three posts with a heading, byline, and summary: one using fragments, one using str.format().

python benchmarks/calls/benchmark.py

Call benchmark (10000 iterations × 5 repeats)
  Fragments:            11.8 µs
  String interpolation:  0.7 µs

Fragments adds overhead over raw string interpolation because it uses the HTML library (el, sequence) to build and join elements safely. For a typical web request this cost is negligible: at 10 µs per render, a single millisecond of request budget can fit ~100 fragment renders.

docs.title: FastAPI Example
docs.text: A small blog application showing how Fragments fits into a real FastAPI project. It covers layout components, component composition, conditional rendering, and list rendering across two routes.

docs.title: Project structure
docs.text:
app/
├── main.py        # FastAPI app and loader registration
├── models.py      # Data model
├── components.py  # Reusable components
└── routes.py      # Route handlers

docs.title: models.py
docs.text:
A plain dataclass to represent a blog post:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class Post:
    slug: str
    title: str
    summary: str
    body: str
    author: str
    published: bool = True

docs.title: components.py
docs.text:
Two components: a full-page layout wrapper, and a post card used in the listing.

from typing import Any
from models import Post

def Layout(children: list[str], title: str = "My Blog") -> str:
    return <>
        <html lang="en">
            <head>
                <meta charset="utf-8" />
                <title>{{ title }}</title>
                <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
                <link rel="stylesheet" href="/static/styles.css" />
            </head>
            <body>
                <header>
                    <a href="/" classes="site-title">My Blog</a>
                </header>
                <main>
                    {{ children }}
                </main>
            </body>
        </html>
    </>


def PostCard(children: list[str], post: Post) -> str:
    return <>
        <article classes="post-card">
            <h2>
                <a href={{ f"/posts/{post.slug}" }}>{{ post.title }}</a>
            </h2>
            <p classes="summary">{{ post.summary }}</p>
            <p classes="byline">By {{ post.author }}</p>
        </article>
    </>

Layout receives title as a keyword argument and children from whatever is nested inside <Layout>...</Layout>. PostCard declares post explicitly as a typed parameter — it ignores children since it's always self-closing.

docs.title: routes.py
docs.text:
Two routes: a post listing and a detail view.

from fastapi import APIRouter, HTTPException
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse
from models import Post
from components import Layout, PostCard

router = APIRouter()

POSTS = [
    Post(
        slug="getting-started",
        title="Getting Started with Fragments",
        summary="How to add Python Fragments to an existing FastAPI app.",
        body="<p>Python Fragments lets you write HTML directly in .py files...</p>",
        author="Alice",
    ),
    Post(
        slug="component-patterns",
        title="Component Patterns",
        summary="Layout wrappers, cards, and other patterns that keep views clean.",
        body="<p>The key insight is that a component is just a function...</p>",
        author="Bob",
    ),
    Post(
        slug="upcoming-features",
        title="Upcoming Features",
        summary="What's coming next.",
        body="",
        author="Alice",
        published=False,
    ),
]

_by_slug: dict[str, Post] = {p.slug: p for p in POSTS}


@router.get("/", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def index() -> str:
    published = [p for p in POSTS if p.published]
    return <>
        <Layout title="My Blog">
            <h1>Latest Posts</h1>
            <PostCard for={{ post in published }} post={{ post }} />
        </Layout>
    </>


@router.get("/posts/{slug}", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def post_detail(slug: str) -> str:
    post = _by_slug.get(slug)
    if post is None or not post.published:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404)
    return <>
        <Layout title={{ post.title }}>
            <article>
                <h1>{{ post.title }}</h1>
                <p classes="byline"><em>By {{ post.author }}</em></p>
                {{ post.body }}
            </article>
            <a href="/" classes="back-link">← All posts</a>
        </Layout>
    </>

The for attribute on <PostCard> renders one card per post, passing each post object through as an attribute. Unpublished posts are filtered in Python before the fragment runs — keeping the markup free of business logic.

docs.title: main.py
docs.text:
Register the loader before any fragment-containing modules are imported:

from fragments import loader  # isort: skip

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse
from routes import router

app = FastAPI()
app.include_router(router)

The # isort: skip comment prevents import sorters from moving the loader import below routes, which would cause fragment syntax to be parsed before the transpiler is active.

docs.title: Getting Started Checklist
docs.text:
• Discover Python Fragments
  • Install the package with pip
  • Install the VS Code extension or LSP
  • Import with from fragments import loader
  • Start writing <>fragments</> in your project

That's it, ready for production.

docs.title: Install the package
docs.text: pip install python-fragments

docs.title: Register the loader
docs.text:
Add this import at your application's entry point, before any modules that contain fragments are imported:

from fragments import loader
from my_components import fragment_component

You may want to include an # isort: skip comment to make sure this import isn't automatically moved.

That's all. Any .py file containing <> will be transpiled automatically at import time.

docs.title: Language Server
docs.text: Type checking, completions, hover docs, go-to-definition, and rename work out of the box.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Diagnostics — type errors and undefined names reported inline
  • Hover — type information and docstrings on hover
  • Completions — attribute, method, and name completions including trigger-on-dot
  • Semantic tokens — type-aware syntax highlighting for Python code inside fragments
  • Syntax highlighting — structural highlighting for <>, tags, and {{ }} interpolations
  • Go-to-definition — jump to the definition of a symbol
  • Find references — list all usages of a symbol
  • Rename — rename a symbol across all files in the workspace

All features work on component names and attribute names in component tags — go-to-definition on <Layout> jumps to the function, rename propagates across all usages, hover shows parameter types.

Not yet implemented:

  • Fragments-specific syntax — autocompletion and syntax highlighting for fragments blocks

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install python-fragments[lsp]

Then start the language server:

fragments-lsp

docs.title: VS Code
docs.text: Type checking, completions, hover docs, go-to-definition, and rename work out of the box.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
For the moment, build and install the extension manually:

npm install -g @vscode/vsce
cd vscode-extension
npm install
vsce package
code --install-extension <filename>.vsix

If a conflicting Python language server (such as Pylance) is active, the extension will prompt you to disable it on first activation.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Setting Default Description fragments.serverPath (derived from active interpreter) Override path to the fragments-lsp executable. Only needed if auto-detection fails.

docs.title: String attributes (default)
docs.text:
Plain string values work exactly as in HTML:

<button type="submit">Save</button>

docs.title: Expression attributes
docs.text:
Use {{ }} to pass any Python expression as an attribute value:

<a href={{ page.url }} id={{ page.slug }}>{{ page.title }}</a>

docs.title: Boolean attributes
docs.text:
Attributes with no value are passed through as-is:

<input type="checkbox" checked />

docs.title: Style
docs.text:
The style attribute accepts a dict. Keys are CSS property names, values are strings:

styles = {"color": "red", "font-weight": "bold"}

return <>
    <p style={{ styles }}>Important</p>
</>

docs.title: Classes
docs.text:
As class is a reserved keyword in Python, use classes instead. It accepts a plain string or a list of strings:

<div classes="card"></div>
<div classes={{ "card" }}></div>
<div classes={{ ["card", "highlighted"] }}></div>
<div classes={{ active_classes }}></div>

docs.title: Comments
docs.text:
HTML comments use the standard <!-- --> syntax and can appear anywhere inside a fragment:

def my_view() -> str:
    return <>
        <!-- page header -->
        <h1>Hello</h1>
        <p>Welcome.</p>
    </>

Comments are passed through to the rendered HTML output unchanged:

<!-- page header --><h1>Hello</h1><p>Welcome.</p>

docs.title: Inside elements
docs.text:
Comments can appear as children of any HTML element:

<ul>
    <!-- items are filtered before rendering -->
    <li for={{ item in published_items }}>{{ item.name }}</li>
</ul>

docs.title: Multiline comments
docs.text:
Comments may span multiple lines:

<>
    <!--
        TODO: replace with a dynamic component once the API is ready
    -->
    <div classes="placeholder">Coming soon</div>
</>

docs.title: Components
docs.text: Any Python function can be used as a component. Components are distinguished from HTML elements by their name: an uppercase first letter means a component call, lowercase means a standard HTML element.

docs.title: Defining a component
docs.text:
A component is a plain Python function that returns a fragment. Attributes passed on the tag become keyword arguments — declare them explicitly or accept them all with **kwargs:

def Card(children: list[str], classes: str = "") -> str:
    return <>
        <div classes={{ classes }}>
            {{ children }}
        </div>
    </>

docs.title: Using a component
docs.text:
Use it like an HTML tag:

return <>
    <Card>
        <p>Some content</p>
    </Card>
</>

Attributes on the tag are forwarded as keyword arguments to the function:

<Card classes="featured">
    <p>Highlighted content</p>
</Card>

docs.title: Self-closing components
docs.text:
Components with no children can use self-closing syntax:

<Avatar src={{ user.avatar_url }} />

docs.title: Conditional rendering
docs.text:
Add an if attribute to any element to render it conditionally:

return <>
    <p if={{ user.is_admin }}>Admin panel</p>
    <p if={{ not user.is_verified }}>Please verify your email.</p>
</>

The element is omitted entirely from the output when the condition is false.

docs.title: Loops
docs.text:
Add a for attribute to repeat an element over any iterable:

return <>
    <ul>
        <li for={{ item in cart.items }}>
            {{ item.name }} — {{ item.price }}
        </li>
    </ul>
</>

if and for can be combined on the same element:

<li for={{ post in posts }} if={{ post.published }}>
    {{ post.title }}
</li>

docs.title: Components
docs.text:
if and for work on component tags too:

<PostCard for={{ post in published }} post={{ post }} />
<Banner if={{ show_banner }} />

They are purely structural — the transpiler handles them before the call is emitted, so neither if nor for is ever passed as a keyword argument to the component function.

docs.title: Fragments
docs.text:
A fragment is a block of HTML written inline in Python, delimited by <> and </>. At runtime, a fragment is transpiled into a function call that returns a plain Python string — so you can use one anywhere you would use a string.

def my_view():
    return <>
        <h1>Hello</h1>
        <p>Welcome.</p>
    </>

Because a fragment evaluates to a string, it can appear as a return value, in an assignment, passed as an argument, or in any other string context:

<> and </> inside Python string literals or line comments are not treated as fragment delimiters, so code like assert result == "<p>hello</p>" or # renders a <> fragment </> is safe.

# As a return value
def my_view() -> str:
    return <>
        <h1>Hello</h1>
    </>

# In an assignment
html = <><p>Some content</p></>

# Passed as an argument
response = HTMLResponse(<><h1>Hello</h1></>)

docs.title: Interpolation
docs.text:
Embed any Python expression inside {{ }}:

def greeting(name: str):
    return <>
        <h1>Hello, {{ name }}!</h1>
        <p>You have {{ len(messages) }} messages.</p>
    </>

The same syntax works in attribute values — see Attributes for details.

docs.title: HTML Library
docs.text:
Fragments are translated into calls to two runtime functions from fragments.html.elements. This is what gives Fragments native HTML awareness.

el(name, children, oneline, attributes) builds a single HTML element.

Argument Type Purpose name str The tag name ("h1", "div", …) children list[str] Rendered inner content oneline bool True for self-closing tags (/>) attributes dict[str, Any] HTML attributes as a dict

sequence(children) concatenates a list of rendered strings into one — it is what <> ... </> compiles to at the top level.

comment(content) renders an HTML comment — it is what <!-- ... --> compiles to.

For example, el("h1",["Hello, world!"],oneline=False,attributes={}) produces the string <h1>Hello, world!</h1>, and sequence([...]) joins a list of elements into the final HTML string.

docs.title: Import Hook
docs.text:
When you import fragments.loader, it installs a custom MetaPathFinder on sys.meta_path. From that point on, every .py file that Python imports is intercepted. If the file contains <>, the source is transpiled before it reaches the interpreter. Files without <> pass through unchanged.

from fragments import loader  # installs the hook — must come first

from fastapi import FastAPI   # any fragment-containing module imported after
from routes import router     # this line is now transpiled automatically

The hook operates on source text, so nothing is written to disk — your .py files are never modified.

docs.title: Transpilation
docs.text: Python does not natively understand <> or HTML tags inside .py files. Fragments solves this with a source-level transpiler that converts fragment syntax into plain Python at import time — so there is no build step.

docs.title: A simple example
docs.text:
Source code

from fragments import loader  # isort: skip

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def index() -> str:
    return <>
        <h1>Hello, world!</h1>
        <p>Welcome to Fragments.</p>
    </>

After transpilation — what the interpreter sees:

from fragments.html.elements import el, sequence, comment
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def index() -> str:
    return sequence([el("h1",["Hello, world!"],oneline=False,attributes={}),el("p",["Welcome to Fragments."],oneline=False,attributes={})])

Endpoint return value — a plain Python string:

'<h1>Hello, world!</h1><p>Welcome to Fragments.</p>'

The transpiler does two things:

  1. Prepends from fragments.html.elements import el, sequence, comment at the top of the file.
  2. Replaces every <> ... </> block with a call to sequence(...).

docs.title: Dynamic content — for and if
docs.text:
The for attribute on an element compiles to a list comprehension, and sequence(...) wraps it so the result is still a flat string.

Before:

@app.get("/posts", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def post_list() -> str:
    posts = get_posts()
    return <>
        <h1>Posts</h1>
        <article for={{ post in posts }}>
            <h2>{{ post.title }}</h2>
            <p>{{ post.summary }}</p>
        </article>
    </>

After:

@app.get("/posts", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def post_list() -> str:
    posts = get_posts()
    return sequence([el("h1",["Posts"],oneline=False,attributes={}),sequence([el("article",[el("h2",[post.title],oneline=False,attributes={}),el("p",[post.summary],oneline=False,attributes={})],oneline=False,attributes={}) for post in posts])])

Result (with two posts in the list):

'<h1>Posts</h1><article><h2>First Post</h2><p>A short summary.</p></article><article><h2>Second Post</h2><p>Another summary.</p></article>'

The for={{ post in posts }} attribute is stripped from the element and becomes the for post in posts clause of a list comprehension. sequence([... for post in posts]) then joins the resulting list of rendered articles.

Similarly, if={{ condition }} compiles to a Python ternary: el(...) if condition else ''.

docs.title: Components — uppercase tags
docs.text:
A tag whose name starts with an uppercase letter is treated as a component call rather than an HTML element. The tag name is used directly as a function reference, and the element's children and attributes are passed as arguments.

Before:

return <>
    <Layout title="My Blog">
        <h1>Posts</h1>
        <PostCard for={{ post in posts }} post={{ post }} />
    </Layout>
</>

After:

return sequence([Layout([el("h1",["Posts"],oneline=False,attributes={}),sequence([PostCard([],post=post) for post in posts])],title="My Blog")])

Layout and PostCard are ordinary Python functions — the transpiler just calls them with children as the first positional argument and tag attributes as keyword arguments. How those kwargs are received is up to the component:

# Accept any attributes with **kwargs
def Layout(children: list[str], **kwargs: Any) -> str: ...

# Or declare each attribute explicitly for type checking
def PostCard(children: list[str], post: Post) -> str: ...

Explicit parameters give you full type checking, refactoring support, and IDE completions with no extra tooling.
